description: Also known as 🌅 Roman Creation Myth.
name: Roman Mythology

keyMyths.title: Chaos and the Separation of Elements
keyMyths.description: Initially, the cosmos was a formless, chaotic mass. A god, or force, separated the elements – earth, air, fire, and water – bringing order and distinct forms to the world. This separation allowed for the creation of the heavens and the earth.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Humans
keyMyths.description: After the separation of elements, the gods, often Prometheus (though Roman sources sometimes attribute it to other deities or natural processes), created humans. Some accounts suggest humans were formed from earth and water, while others propose they emerged from the gods' divine seeds.

keyMyths.title: The Ages of Man
keyMyths.description: Roman mythology, influenced by Greek thought, describes a succession of ages, each characterized by different moral and societal conditions. These ages typically include a Golden Age of peace and abundance, followed by Silver, Bronze, and Iron Ages, each progressively more corrupt and violent.

keyMyths.title: The War of the Titans (Gigantomachy)
keyMyths.description: The Roman Gigantomachy, a war between the Olympian gods and the Giants (Giantes), parallels the Greek Titanomachy. This conflict represents the establishment of the Olympian order and the defeat of primordial forces that threatened the new cosmic structure.

keyMyths.title: The Flood of Deucalion and Pyrrha
keyMyths.description: A great flood, sent by Jupiter (Zeus in Greek mythology), destroyed almost all of humanity as punishment for their wickedness. Deucalion and Pyrrha, warned by Prometheus, survived and repopulated the earth by casting stones behind them, which transformed into people.

sources.name: Ovid, Metamorphoses
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of myths and legends, providing a crucial source for Roman cosmology and creation stories.
sources.title: Ovid, Metamorphoses

sources.name: Virgil, Aeneid
sources.description: While primarily an epic poem, the Aeneid offers insights into Roman religious beliefs and the divine order of the world.
sources.title: Virgil, Aeneid

sources.name: Hyginus, Fabulae
sources.description: A collection of mythological summaries and stories, valuable for understanding Roman interpretations of Greek myths.
sources.title: Hyginus, Fabulae

sources.name: Livy, Ab Urbe Condita
sources.description: Livy's history of Rome from its founding includes accounts of religious practices and beliefs that shaped the Roman worldview.
sources.title: Livy, Ab Urbe Condita

sources.name: Cicero, De Natura Deorum
sources.description: A philosophical dialogue exploring Roman views on the nature of the gods and their role in the world.
sources.title: Cicero, De Natura Deorum
